questions.question: Explain the five implementation levels of virtualization with examples for each level.
keyPoints: ISA Level: Emulates one instruction set on another (QEMU, Bochs). Highest flexibility, lowest performance.
HAL Level: Hypervisor virtualizes hardware for multiple VMs (VMware ESXi, Xen, KVM). Good performance with hardware assist.
OS Level: Single kernel supports isolated containers (Docker, LXC, Solaris Zones). Best performance, shares kernel.
Library Level: Intercepts and redirects API calls (Wine, vCUDA). Limited to compatible API sets.
Application Level: Virtual runtime for bytecode execution (JVM, .NET CLR). Write once, run anywhere.

questions.question: Compare OS-level virtualization with HAL-level virtualization in terms of performance, isolation, and use cases.
keyPoints: OS-level shares host kernel (lightweight), HAL-level runs separate OS per VM (heavier)
OS-level offers near-native performance, HAL-level has moderate overhead from hypervisor
HAL-level provides stronger isolation (separate kernels), OS-level shares kernel (weaker isolation)
OS-level used for microservices and containers, HAL-level used for IaaS and server consolidation
OS-level containers start in milliseconds, HAL-level VMs take seconds to minutes
